---
title: "Context Management"
subtitle: "Project instructions, skills, and when to use them"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
---

# Try This First: Context A/B

Run the same task twice and compare what changes when the folder contains a context file.

- [Without context file](context-ab/without-context/visualization-task.html)
- [With context file](context-ab/with-context/visualization-task.html)

Use the same agent, same model, and same prompt as much as possible.

```text
Complete the visualization task in this notebook. Use simple R code. Do not make causal claims. After editing, explain your choices and what still needs human review.
```

After both runs, compare:

- Did the agent follow the requested plot style?
- Did it use readable labels and units?
- Did it add a subtitle and caption?
- Did it avoid causal language?
- Did it explain what it changed?

# What Changed?

Agents are different from chatbots because they can inspect files, edit files, and run commands. That means the project folder itself becomes part of the prompt.

In the A/B exercise, the `with-context` folder contains an `AGENTS.md` file. The `without-context` folder lacks that file. The file gives the agent durable instructions without making you paste those instructions into every prompt.

# Context Files

Context files are plain text instruction files stored in a project. Different tools look for different names.

| File | Commonly read by | Typical scope |
|:-----|:-----------------|:--------------|
| `CLAUDE.md` | Claude Code, OpenCode | Project or directory |
| `AGENTS.md` | Codex, OpenCode, emerging standard | Project or directory |
| `.cursorrules` | Cursor | Project root |

Many agents also infer context by reading files such as `README.md`, `_quarto.yml`, `pyproject.toml`, `DESCRIPTION`, `renv.lock`, `package.json`, or source code. Context files should not duplicate all of that documentation. They should state the rules the agent is unlikely to infer reliably.

# What Belongs In A Context File

Use context files for durable, high-signal instructions.

Good candidates:

- project purpose and audience
- required workflow steps
- file and directory conventions
- package or language rules
- privacy and data handling constraints
- testing, rendering, or verification commands
- style rules that matter for the work
- instructions about what not to change

Poor candidates:

- vague advice such as "be rigorous" or "write clean code"
- copied documentation the agent can read elsewhere
- huge lists of preferences that rarely matter
- instructions that conflict with other files
- sensitive information, credentials, or private data

# Evidence And Caution

Context files help, but they cannot guarantee good output.

Gloaguen, Mundler, Muller, Raychev, and Vechev (2026) evaluated `AGENTS.md` files on SWE-bench tasks and found that AI-generated repository context files often reduced agent success rates and increased cost. Hand-written context files performed better.

Practical takeaway:

- write context files yourself
- keep them short
- make rules concrete and testable
- remove stale instructions
- do not treat the file as a safety guarantee

A context file can tell an agent not to overwrite data, expose PHI, or make unsupported causal claims. You still need to review what the agent does.

# A Portable Pattern

A useful context file often has five parts.

## Context

Briefly explain the project, audience, and environment.

## Rules

List concrete requirements the agent should follow.

## Examples

Show one or two examples of correct behavior.

## Anti-Patterns

State the common mistakes to avoid.

## References

Point to the real source of truth, such as a style guide, data dictionary, assignment prompt, or analysis plan.

# Example: Analysis Project Context

```markdown
# AGENTS.md

## Context

This is a teaching project for an applied biostatistics course. Students are learning reproducible AI-assisted workflows in R and Quarto.

## Rules

- Use relative file paths from the project root.
- Prefer simple, readable R code that students can inspect.
- Use `read.csv()` or `readr::read_csv()` consistently within a single file.
- Do not make causal claims from observational summaries.
- Keep generated code simple enough for students to inspect.
- After editing a `.qmd` file, render it and report any errors.

## Examples

- Good: "People with higher exposure had higher observed outcome values in this sample."
- Bad: "The exposure caused the outcome to increase."

## Anti-Patterns

- Do not add new packages unless the assignment requires them.
- Do not overwrite files in `data/real/`.
- Do not paste or summarize private data into cloud tools.

## References

- Course setup: `README.md`
- Data dictionary: `data/codebooks/`
- Assignment instructions: `assignments/`
```

# Skills

Skills are reusable, task-specific instruction bundles for agents. They are useful when the same workflow comes up repeatedly across projects or sessions.

Examples:

- review an R analysis for statistical interpretation errors
- convert a messy script into a reproducible Quarto notebook
- check a visualization against a publication style guide
- audit a simulated-data workflow before code touches real data

Anthropic's skill format usually includes a `SKILL.md` file with frontmatter plus instructions. A minimal skill looks like this:

```markdown
---
name: analysis-review
description: Review an applied statistical analysis for reproducibility, statistical interpretation, and verification gaps.
---

Use this skill when reviewing R, Python, Stata, or SAS analysis code.

Check for:
- unsupported causal language
- missing data handling
- incorrect model interpretation
- unverified AI-generated code
- outputs that are not reproducible from source files
```

Skills are still a developing practice. Agents may not always invoke them when you expect, and invoked skills add context cost. Use skills for recurring workflows, not one-off project facts.

# Context File Or Skill?

Use this rule of thumb.

| Need | Better fit |
|:-----|:-----------|
| Project-specific rules | `AGENTS.md` or `CLAUDE.md` |
| Directory-specific behavior | nested `AGENTS.md` or `CLAUDE.md` |
| Reusable workflow across projects | skill |
| One-time task instruction | prompt |
| Factual project documentation | `README.md`, codebook, or analysis plan |

# Reflection

Answer briefly:

- What instruction helped the agent most?
- What instruction did the agent ignore or over-apply?
- What should be in a project context file for your own work?
- What should stay in a one-time prompt instead?

# References

- Gloaguen, T., Mundler, N., Muller, M., Raychev, V., & Vechev, M. (2026). Evaluating AGENTS.md: Are repository-level context files helpful for coding agents? <https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988>
- Anthropic Skills examples: <https://github.com/anthropics/skills>
- Laravel Skills documentation: <https://skills.laravel.cloud/docs>
